title: Optimistic forecasts questioned
full_text:
Treasury's expectation that living standards will improve and real wage growth will return next year is predicated on inflation forecasts that are more optimistic than those published by the Reserve Bank of Australia, as real wage growth has gone backwards for the second quarter in a row.
New data released on Wednesday showed wages in the Australian economy grew 3.3 per cent over the year to the March quarter. But headline inflation - which averaged 4.1 per cent over the March quarter - eroded workers' pay packets, resulting in negative real wage growth.
In his post-budget National Press Club address in Canberra, Treasurer Jim Chalmers pushed back against private sector economists who said the budget added to Australia's inflation challenge in the near-term.
"The budget is taking the pressure off inflation ... The underlying cash balance each year [for the next five years] is better than what it was in December," Chalmers said.
But Commonwealth Bank of Australia chief economist Luke Yeaman said the budget was "a missed opportunity" to help the RBA fight inflation since it was "neutral to mildly expansionary" next financial year.
Active policy decisions taken by the government will inject an extra $6.5 billion into the economy next year.
Yeaman said this "small fiscal loosening" added to the risks the RBA would deliver a fourth rate rise this year. "Less spending would have given the RBA more breathing room, reducing the likelihood of further interest rate hikes if inflation remains high," he said.
Updated economic forecasts released in Tuesday's budget showed Treasury expected inflation to be 5 per cent over 2025-26 before falling to 2.5 per cent by 2026-27 - the midpoint of the RBA's 2 per cent to 3 per cent target band.
HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham said Treasury's forecasts "all hang together as a very optimistic set of numbers". He said the inflation forecasts were probably "assuming optimal policy will be delivered and inflation will go back to the RBA's target".
"It's sort of an acknowledgement that the RBA, over that time horizon, is expected to achieve its price stability mandate," he said.
Treasury's optimistic inflation forecasts underpin the department's expectation that real wage growth will return in 2026-27.
It forecast the wage price index to grow 3.5 per cent in both 2026-27 and 2027-28. But the Reserve Bank forecast wage growth to be 3.2 per cent until June 2027, when it expects it to fall slightly to 3.0 per cent.
Treasury's inflation forecast is more optimistic than the RBA's, updated last week, which showed headline inflation still at 4 per cent in December this year before declining to 2.4 per cent by the middle of next year.
Government spending in the near-term is driven by funding to the states and territories for hospital services, defence spending, and additions to the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme. AMP chief economist Shane Oliver said while Chalmers' fifth budget was more responsible than the past few because it was the first to see net budget savings over the next five years, spending cuts were less than hoped and remained "higher than normal".
Over the medium term, federal government spending as a share of GDP is projected to fall from 26.9 per cent to 26.5 per cent - still above its pre-pandemic average of 23.5 per cent.
"This leaves little room for a pick-up in private spending in the economy without hitting capacity constraints and higher inflation," Oliver said.
The risk, Oliver said, was that the government would lose its appetite to rein in spending in its next budget, which will be Labor's last before the 2028 federal election.
"[The] pressure will be once again to ramp up spending," he said.
summary: Treasury's optimistic forecasts for wage growth and inflation are being questioned as real wages fall and inflation remains high. Economists warn that government spending could complicate efforts to control inflation and interest rates.
author: Lea Jurkovic | Economics correspond
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Treasurer Jim Chalmers defends budget against inflation concerns.
Real wage growth has declined for two consecutive quarters.
Commonwealth Bank's economist calls budget a 'missed opportunity' for inflation control.
Treasury forecasts inflation to fall to 2.5% by 2026-27.
Government spending is projected to remain high despite budget savings.
ticker_line: Treasury's wage growth forecasts face scrutiny
